resume:
Dr. Aileen Koh — Director of Research

Leading 一个 40-people 的 ML research org，方向是 foundation models 的 safety & alignment。

Now: Director, Research — Veridian AI Labs (2022–present)
Before: Principal Scientist — Helix Research (2016–2022)
PhD, Machine Learning。

Highlights:
- 带过 8 个 research scientists 和 2 个 research managers
- Set 了 lab 的 multi-year research agenda
- Published 在 NeurIPS / ICML；h-index 30+
- 负责 research-to-product 的 transfer

联系方式略。
jd: Veridian AI Labs is hiring a Director of Research to own the strategy and execution of a multi-team research organization (30–50 researchers) working on alignment and evaluation of frontier models. You will set the multi-year research agenda, make portfolio bets across exploratory and applied tracks, hire and grow research managers, and translate research into product-shippable capabilities. The role reports to the VP of Research and is accountable for both scientific impact (publications, benchmarks) and concrete transfer into the product roadmap. We are looking for a leader who can make hard prioritization calls under deep uncertainty, defend why one bet was funded over another, and own the outcome when a bet does not pay off.

history.q: You've set the multi-year research agenda at Veridian. Walk me through one specific bet you funded and one you explicitly killed.
Let's stay on the one you killed. Whose project was it, and how did you personally deliver that decision?
Looking back, what's the single biggest research bet you got wrong, and what did it cost the org?

candidate_last_answer: That's a fair question, but before I answer it I'm curious — how does your org actually define a research bet being 『wrong』? Because in my experience the framing matters enormously. Do you count a negative result that saved us from a dead end as a failure, or as a successful de-risking? And when you ask what it 『cost』, are you measuring researcher-months, opportunity cost against the roadmap, or reputational cost externally? I find that how a company answers that tells me more about whether I'd thrive there than any single story I could give you. So maybe you tell me how Veridian thinks about that, and I'll map my example onto your definition.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: how does your org actually define a research bet being 『wrong』

expected-yield: redirect the candidate off the meta-discussion onto a concrete bet that did not pay off — what they believed at funding time, what evidence later proved them wrong, the researcher-months sunk, and what they would have decided differently
